title: Microeconomics Fundamentals
subtitle: Markets, behaviour, and equilibrium
description: A full semester-equivalent academic course on microeconomic theory — supply, demand, elasticity, market structures, and welfare analysis.
targetAudience: Undergraduate economics, business, and public policy students
objectives: Derive and interpret supply and demand curves
Compute price, income, and cross-price elasticities
Analyse market outcomes under perfect competition, monopoly, and oligopoly
Evaluate welfare effects of taxes, subsidies, and price controls
Apply marginal analysis to consumer and producer decisions

modules.title: Supply and Demand
modules.description: The foundational model of market interaction.
objectives: Trace effects of shifts in each curve

lessons.title: The Demand Curve
lessons.description: Quantity demanded as a function of price.
lessons.content: The demand curve plots quantity demanded against price, holding other variables constant. Its downward slope reflects both the substitution effect and the income effect of a price change.
keyPoints: Movement along vs shift of the curve are distinct phenomena
Ceteris paribus assumptions are explicit, not decorative
Individual demand aggregates to market demand horizontally

lessons.title: The Supply Curve
lessons.description: Quantity supplied as a function of price.
lessons.content: The supply curve reflects producer decisions given costs of production. Its upward slope follows from increasing marginal costs at higher output levels.
keyPoints: Short-run and long-run supply differ in which inputs are variable
Technology shifts the supply curve; price changes move along it
Market supply is the horizontal sum of firm supplies

lessons.title: Equilibrium
lessons.description: The price at which plans are consistent.
lessons.content: Market equilibrium is the price-quantity pair at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. Deviations generate either surplus or shortage, with self-correcting pressure back to equilibrium.
keyPoints: Equilibrium is a tendency, not a steady observation
Disequilibrium implies either surplus or shortage
Price adjustment speed depends on market structure

lessons.title: Comparative Statics
lessons.description: Tracing the effects of parameter changes.
lessons.content: Comparative statics compares equilibria before and after a parameter change — an input-price increase, a tax, a technology shock — without modelling the adjustment path.
keyPoints: Shifts of curves cause new equilibria
Direction of change is often unambiguous; magnitude needs elasticities
The comparative-statics method ignores adjustment dynamics deliberately

quiz.question: An increase in consumer income shifts the demand curve for a normal good in which direction?
quiz.explanation: A normal good is defined by positive income elasticity — higher income increases demand at every price, shifting the curve rightward.

lessons.title: Price Elasticity of Demand
lessons.content: Price elasticity of demand is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price. Values above one in absolute magnitude indicate elastic demand; below one, inelastic.
keyPoints: Elasticity varies along a linear demand curve
Arc elasticity uses midpoints to avoid directional asymmetry
Total revenue increases with price cuts only on the elastic segment

lessons.title: Income and Cross-Price
lessons.description: Responsiveness to income and to other goods' prices.
lessons.content: Income elasticity distinguishes normal from inferior goods; cross-price elasticity distinguishes substitutes from complements.
keyPoints: Positive income elasticity → normal good; negative → inferior
Positive cross-price → substitutes; negative → complements
Magnitudes identify luxuries vs necessities and close vs distant substitutes

lessons.title: Applications
lessons.content: Elasticities determine who bears the economic burden of a tax — the side of the market with more inelastic behaviour absorbs more of it, regardless of which side writes the cheque.
keyPoints: Statutory incidence differs from economic incidence
The more inelastic side bears more of the tax burden
Elasticity-based pricing is standard practice in monopoly settings

lessons.title: Preferences and Utility
lessons.content: Standard consumer theory assumes preferences are complete, transitive, and monotonic. A utility function is a numerical representation that preserves the preference ordering.
keyPoints: Ordinal utility is all the theory requires
Indifference curves are level sets of the utility function
Convex preferences imply diminishing marginal rates of substitution

lessons.title: The Budget Constraint
lessons.content: The budget line represents all bundles costing exactly the consumer's income, given market prices. Its slope equals the negative price ratio.
keyPoints: Price changes rotate the line; income changes shift it
The feasible set is the triangle below the budget line
The budget line is the relevant frontier for optimisation

lessons.title: Utility Maximisation
lessons.content: The optimal bundle lies where an indifference curve is tangent to the budget line — the consumer's marginal rate of substitution equals the market's price ratio.
keyPoints: Interior optima satisfy MRS = price ratio
Corner solutions violate tangency but are still optimal
Lagrangian methods handle the constrained optimisation formally

modules.title: Market Structures
objectives: Characterise the four canonical market structures

lessons.title: Perfect Competition
lessons.description: Price-taking firms and zero long-run profit.
lessons.content: Perfectly competitive firms take price as given and produce where marginal cost equals price. Free entry drives economic profit to zero in the long run.
keyPoints: Firms are price-takers because each is small relative to market
Short-run shutdown decision uses average variable cost
Long-run equilibrium occurs at minimum long-run average cost

lessons.title: Monopoly
lessons.content: A monopolist faces the entire market demand curve and sets quantity where marginal revenue equals marginal cost — producing less and charging more than a competitive equivalent.
keyPoints: Marginal revenue is below price because of the demand-curve slope
Deadweight loss measures welfare reduction vs competitive outcome
Price discrimination can partly recover lost welfare — or worsen it

lessons.title: Oligopoly
lessons.content: With only a handful of firms, each must anticipate rivals' responses. Cournot and Bertrand models differ in whether firms choose quantity or price as their strategic variable.
keyPoints: Nash equilibrium is the standard solution concept
Cournot quantity competition yields outcomes between monopoly and competition
Bertrand price competition can produce perfectly competitive outcomes with just two firms

modules.title: Welfare and Market Failure
modules.description: When markets work and when they don't.

lessons.title: Surplus and Efficiency
lessons.content: Consumer surplus measures willingness to pay above market price; producer surplus measures revenue above marginal cost. Their sum is maximised in the competitive equilibrium under standard assumptions.
keyPoints: Surplus areas are geometric on the supply-demand diagram
The first welfare theorem formalises the efficiency result
Distributional implications are separate from efficiency claims

lessons.title: Externalities and Public Goods
lessons.content: Externalities arise when one agent's decisions affect others without compensation; public goods are non-rival and non-excludable. Both produce equilibria that diverge from the socially optimal outcome.
keyPoints: Pigouvian taxes internalise external costs
Public goods suffer free-rider problems in private provision
Coasian bargaining can resolve small-scale externalities when property rights are clear

quiz.question: A Pigouvian tax is designed to internalise which of the following?
options: External costs imposed on third parties
quiz.explanation: Pigouvian taxes target negative externalities by making producers pay the social cost of the external harm they impose.
